skill.name: file-security-toolkit
skill.description: Encrypt/decrypt local files, redact sensitive information in documents, and validate password strength when handling private data or preparing files for sharing.
skill.summary: Encrypt, decrypt, redact, and validate password strength for local file security operations.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate file security toolkit for encryption, decryption, and document redaction. The static scanner risk score of 100/100 was inflated by aggressive keyword matching: 26 'ransomware keyword' flags are false positives triggered by legitimate encryption terminology, 45 'shell backtick execution' flags are Markdown code formatting, and 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags are false positives against standard libraries (cryptography.fernet, PBKDF2HMAC). One confirmed finding is a zip slip vulnerability in the extractall() call at scripts/file_security.py:114, which requires a warning but does not indicate malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Archive Extraction (Zip Slip)
medium_findings.description: The zip_decrypt function uses extractall() without validating that extracted file paths stay within the target output directory. A crafted ZIP archive containing path traversal filenames (e.g., '../../evil.txt') could write files outside the intended extraction directory. While the skill is legitimate and operates locally, this is a real vulnerability if a user processes an untrusted archive.

low_findings.title: Password Visible in Process List
low_findings.description: Passwords are accepted via the --password CLI argument, making them visible to other users through system process listings (ps aux) and shell history files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Archive Extraction via extractall
dangerous_patterns.description: The zip_decrypt pattern calls AESZipFile.extractall(output_path) without sanitizing extracted paths. An attacker who crafts a ZIP with path traversal entries could achieve arbitrary file write outside the target directory.

content.user_title: Encrypt, Decrypt, and Redact Files Securely
content.value_statement: Protecting sensitive data in local files requires reliable encryption and redaction tools that do not send data over the network. This skill provides a packaged workflow for encrypting files and folders with AES-256, redacting personal information from documents, and validating password strength.
actual_capabilities: Encrypt single files or entire folders using AES-256 with password-based key derivation and store them as password-protected ZIP archives.
Decrypt previously encrypted files and ZIP archives back to their original form.
Redact email addresses, phone numbers, ID numbers, and name or address keywords from TXT, MD, CSV, DOCX, and PPTX files.
Validate whether a password meets minimum strength requirements including length, uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and digits.
Process all file operations entirely on the local machine with no network access or data transmission.
limitations: Redaction uses regular expression pattern matching and may miss context-specific or non-standard sensitive information.
Password strength check uses basic composition rules and does not verify against databases of known compromised passwords.
Encrypted output uses a custom format that is not interoperable with standard encryption tools like GPG or OpenSSL.
Redaction does not support PDF files or image-based document formats.

use_cases.title: Prepare Documents for Public Sharing
use_cases.description: A researcher redacts personal information from survey data files before publishing a dataset for public use.

use_cases.title: Secure Sensitive Files Before Transfer
use_cases.description: An analyst encrypts a folder of confidential reports into an AES-256 protected archive before sending them to a colleague.

use_cases.title: Automate Password Validation in Pipelines
use_cases.description: A developer integrates password strength checking into an automated data processing workflow to enforce minimum security policies.

prompt_templates.title: Encrypt a Folder of Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Encrypt all files in the folder ./reports using AES-256 and save the archive as ./reports.zip with a strong password.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to protect a folder of documents before sharing.

prompt_templates.title: Redact Personal Information from a Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Redact all email addresses, phone numbers, and ID numbers from the file ./data/survey_results.docx and save the output to ./data/survey_results.redacted.docx.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs to remove personal details from a document before distribution.

prompt_templates.title: Redact and Encrypt Multiple Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Process all .docx files in ./incoming by redacting personal information, then encrypt each result into a password-protected ZIP archive saved to ./outgoing.
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a batch of documents that need both redaction and encryption.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Password Before Encryption
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether the password meets the minimum strength requirements (at least 8 characters, includes uppercase, lowercase, and a digit). If it passes, use it to encrypt ./data.txt to ./data.txt.enc.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to verify password strength before encrypting a file.

output_examples.input: Encrypt the folder ./project-docs with password protection.
output_examples.output: The script created ./project-docs.zip with AES-256 encryption. The original folder was not modified. Password verification passed before encryption.

Processed contact_list.csv and saved redacted output to contact_list.redacted.csv. Found and masked 12 email addresses, 8 phone numbers, and 5 address entries. Review the output to confirm all sensitive data was removed.

output_examples.input: Decrypt the file report.pdf.enc with the provided password.
output_examples.output: Decryption completed successfully. The decrypted file was saved as report.pdf. The original encrypted file was not modified.

best_practices: Always verify the redacted output file to confirm all sensitive information was properly masked before sharing or publishing.
Use strong passwords that include uppercase letters, lowercase letters, digits, and special characters for encryption operations.
Keep backup copies of original files before running encryption or redaction operations in case the output needs to be regenerated.
anti_patterns: Storing passwords inside the script file or in version control instead of providing them securely at runtime.
Using weak or reused passwords for encryption that do not meet the minimum strength requirements.
Sharing encrypted files without documenting the decryption password through a separate secure channel.

faq.question: What file formats does the redaction feature support?
faq.answer: Redaction supports TXT, MD, CSV, DOCX, and PPTX files.

faq.question: Does this skill send my data over the network?
faq.answer: No. All processing happens locally on your machine with no network access or data transmission.

faq.question: What encryption algorithm does this skill use?
faq.answer: ZIP archives use AES-256 encryption. Single-file encryption uses Fernet (AES-128-CBC with HMAC) with PBKDF2 key derivation.

faq.question: Can I decrypt files created by other encryption tools?
faq.answer: No. The encrypted format is specific to this toolkit and is not compatible with standard encryption tools.

faq.question: How does the password strength check work?
faq.answer: The check verifies minimum length of 8 characters and requires at least one uppercase letter, one lowercase letter, and one digit.

faq.question: Can I process multiple files in one operation?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the zip-encrypt command to encrypt multiple files or an entire folder into a single AES-256 protected ZIP archive.
